posts.text: possibly the high water mark of whimsical virality, it’s all been downhill since: “Around St. Patrick’s Day in 2006, a morning news program in Mobile​, Ala., aired a story​ that turned heads: Someone claimed to have spotted a leprechaun…” nytimes.com/2026/03/17/us/mo…

“Practically overnight, we took an ancient vice—long regarded as soul-rotting and civilizationally ruinous—put it on everyone’s phone, and made it as normal and frictionless as checking the weather. What could possibly go wrong?” theatlantic.com/magazine/202…

this caricature of Jackson as an anti-white, anti-Semitic demagogue never reflected the man. The entire point of Jackson’s “Rainbow Coalition,” his vision of Americans from all backgrounds coming together for social justice, was overcoming such differences theatlantic.com/ideas/2026/0…

Here’s the close of Jesse’s 1988 DNC speech “keep hope alive.” The challenge for politicians on the left remains unsparing critique that doesn’t descend into cynicism. There’s a reason that churched, black political figures navigate this more seamlessly than others

Video

the fruit of Jesse’s labor is apparent in so much of what comes after him: not just the rise of Obama, but also of Bernie and the current multi-racial progressive politics. He was the crucial bridge between 68 and our contemporary moment

over decades, Jackson: insisted on a multiracial coalition, poured material resources into enfranchising southern voters, helped figure out what civil rights looks like in northern urban contexts, and helped define the lane for black leaders within national D politics

in 1968, the movement faces a number of challenges: the rise of more militant black power, the waning utility of southern campaigns the hostile terrain of the North, the question of how to now use voting rights to build electoral power…

there’s a barely-concealed condescension that frames (white) mainstream discussion of Jesse Jackson - he was “complex”, “personal failings”, “self promotion” “ego” - that, functionally, denies him his rightful mantle: possibly the most important political figure of his era

after a three-day march across North Carolina, @RevDrBarber rallied a few thousand people outside of the statehouse this morning - telling the story of Claudius and St. Valentine @rolandsmartin @BlkStarNetwork
